question: At about what air tank pressure does the governor stop the compressor from pumping air (cut-out)?
explanation: When tank pressure rises to the cut-out level (around 125 psi), the governor stops the compressor from pumping air.

question: At about what tank pressure does the governor allow the compressor to start pumping again (cut-in)?
explanation: When tank pressure falls to the cut-in pressure (around 100 psi), the governor allows the compressor to start pumping again.

question: At what pressure is the safety relief valve usually set to open?
explanation: The safety relief valve is usually set to open at 150 psi to protect the system from too much pressure.

question: A visible low air pressure warning signal must come on before tank pressure falls below what level?
explanation: A warning signal you can see must come on before the air pressure in the tanks falls below 60 psi.

question: At what pressure does a wig wag device drop a mechanical arm into the driver's view?
explanation: The wig wag drops a mechanical arm into your view when system pressure drops below 60 psi.

question: Tractor and straight truck spring brakes come fully on when air pressure drops to what range?
explanation: Tractor and straight truck spring brakes will come fully on when air pressure drops to a range of 20 to 45 psi (typically 20 to 30 psi).

question: Automatic front wheel limiting valves stop reducing air to the front brakes when application pressure reaches about what level?
explanation: Automatic front wheel limiting valves reduce air to the front brakes except when the brakes are applied very hard (60 psi or more application pressure).

question: Before driving a dual air system vehicle, the compressor should build a minimum of what pressure in both systems?
explanation: Allow time for the air compressor to build up a minimum of 100 psi pressure in both the primary and secondary systems before driving.

question: In a dual air brake system, the warning light and buzzer should come on before air pressure drops below what level in either system?
explanation: The warning light and buzzer should come on before the air pressure drops below 60 psi in either system.

question: When pulling on a slack adjuster, about how much movement indicates it probably needs adjustment?
choices: More than about one quarter inch
More than about one half inch
explanation: If a slack adjuster moves more than about one inch where the push rod attaches, it probably needs adjustment.

question: Brake drums or discs must not have cracks longer than what fraction of the friction area width?
explanation: Brake drums or discs must not have cracks longer than one half the width of the friction area.

topic: Leakage rate engine off, no brake
question: With the engine off and parking brake released, the air loss rate should be less than what for a single vehicle?
explanation: The loss rate should be less than two psi in one minute for single vehicles and less than three psi for combination vehicles.

question: With the foot brake fully applied for one minute, the air pressure should not drop more than what for a single vehicle?
explanation: With the foot brake fully applied, air pressure dropping more than three psi in one minute (single vehicle) means the air loss rate is too much.

question: During the air brake check, the parking brake valve should pop out causing spring brakes to come on when pressure falls into what range?
explanation: The parking brake valve should close (pop out) and spring brakes come on when air pressure falls to the manufacturer's specification of 20 to 45 psi.

question: In dual air systems at operating rpm, pressure should build from 85 to 100 psi within how long?
explanation: In dual air systems, pressure should build from 85 to 100 psi within 45 seconds when the engine is at operating rpms.

question: When using stab braking, when should you reapply the brakes after releasing them?
choices: As soon as the wheels start rolling again
After holding the release for five seconds
Only after the vehicle fully straightens
explanation: In stab braking you release brakes when wheels lock up and reapply them fully as soon as the wheels start rolling again.

question: About how much distance does air brake lag add at 55 mph on dry pavement?
explanation: The air brake lag distance at 55 mph on dry pavement adds about 32 feet.

question: Using proper braking technique on a downgrade, when should you release the brakes after slowing?
choices: When speed is reduced to about 5 mph below the safe speed
When speed reaches the safe speed exactly
When speed is 10 mph above the safe speed
explanation: Release the brakes when your speed has been reduced to approximately five mph below your safe speed.

question: At what air pressure do spring brakes automatically come on?
explanation: Spring brakes automatically activate when air pressure drops to 20-45 psi (typically 20-30 psi).

question: What is the minimum air pressure required in both systems before you can drive a dual air brake system vehicle?
explanation: You must have a minimum of 100 psi in both the primary and secondary air systems before driving.

question: What is the purpose of an alcohol evaporator in an air brake system?
choices: To cool the air before braking
To reduce ice buildup in the brake system in cold weather
explanation: Alcohol evaporators are used in cold weather to reduce ice buildup inside the air brake system.

question: Before what pressure level must the low air pressure warning light come on?
explanation: The low air pressure warning must come on before the pressure drops below 60 psi.

question: If your vehicle does not have automatic air tank drains, when should you drain the tanks?
choices: At the end of each working day
Only when low air warning comes on
explanation: If the vehicle does not have automatic drains, air tanks should be drained at the end of each working day to remove moisture and oil.

question: On newer air-brake vehicles, how do you apply spring parking brakes with the yellow knob?
explanation: On newer vehicles, the yellow diamond-shaped push-pull control is pulled out to apply parking brakes and pushed in to release.

question: Why should you avoid pressing the brake pedal when spring brakes are applied?
choices: It can damage brakes from combined spring and air forces
explanation: Pressing the pedal with spring brakes on can damage components due to combined spring force and air pressure.

question: What is the main cause of brake fade on long downgrades?
choices: Overheating from excessive service brake use
explanation: Brake fade is mainly caused by overheating from overusing service brakes instead of relying on proper gear and engine braking.

question: After slowing about 5 mph below safe speed on a downgrade, what should you do next?
choices: Release brakes and let speed rise back to safe speed before reapplying
explanation: Proper downgrade technique is to apply firm braking to reduce speed, release, then reapply when speed returns to the chosen safe speed.

question: If ABS malfunctions, what braking capability remains?
choices: Normal brakes still work, but ABS assist is lost
explanation: ABS is an added safety system. If it fails, regular braking remains available, though wheel-lock control is reduced.

question: When should parking brakes be avoided temporarily?
choices: When brakes are very hot or very wet in freezing temperatures
explanation: Very hot brakes can be damaged if set immediately, and very wet brakes in freezing conditions can freeze. Use chocks when needed.

question: What does the air supply pressure gauge tell you?
choices: How hard you are pressing the brake pedal
How much pressure is in the air tank(s)
How much pressure is in each tire
How hot the brake drums are
explanation: Supply pressure gauges show the available air pressure in the reservoir tank(s).

question: How can you verify whether a vehicle is equipped with ABS?
choices: Check the certification label manufacturing date and ABS indicator lamps
Look for a blue parking brake knob
explanation: The guide points to certification date and ABS malfunction lamps as key indicators of ABS equipment.

question: What is a dual air brake system?
choices: Two pedals controlling front and rear brakes separately
Two separate air systems sharing one set of brake controls for safety
A system used only on trailers
explanation: A dual air system has primary and secondary circuits with separate tanks/lines for safety redundancy.

question: How do you test that the parking brake will hold?
choices: Set parking brake, then gently pull against it in a low gear
explanation: Section 5.3.3 says to apply the parking brake and gently pull against it in low gear to verify holding power.

question: During final checks, how should service brakes be tested?
choices: Move slowly (about 5 mph) and brake firmly, checking for pull or delayed stopping
explanation: Guide procedure calls for a low-speed, firm brake test to detect pull, unusual feel, or delay.

question: What is controlled braking in an emergency?
choices: Brake as hard as possible without locking wheels, releasing if lockup occurs
explanation: Controlled braking uses strong brake pressure while avoiding wheel lock so steering control can be maintained.

question: When driving a tractor-trailer with ABS, what basic braking approach is recommended?
choices: Brake differently depending on which unit has ABS
Brake normally and monitor tractor/trailer stability
explanation: The guide says to brake the same way as usual, using only the force needed and monitoring stability.

question: When is full brake application in an emergency specifically allowed by the guide?
choices: Any vehicle with at least one ABS axle
Only if trailer ABS lamp is off
If you always drive a straight truck or combination with working ABS on all axles
explanation: Section 5.4.2 gives one exception: with working ABS on all axles (and that operating context), emergency full application is acceptable.

question: What should you do before leaving an air-brake vehicle unattended?
choices: Apply parking brakes or chock wheels
explanation: Never leave the vehicle unattended without applying parking brakes or using chocks to prevent rollaway.

question: What triggers the brake lights in an air brake system?
choices: A mechanical linkage at each wheel
An electric switch activated by air pressure
The parking brake knob position only
explanation: The stop-light switch is air-pressure operated and turns on brake lights when air brakes are applied.

question: If the application pressure gauge shows you need more pressure to hold the same speed, what is a likely problem?
choices: Brakes may be fading or out of adjustment
explanation: Needing increasing application pressure for the same effect can indicate brake fade, adjustment issues, or leaks/mechanical faults.

question: Why should you avoid pressing and releasing the brake pedal unnecessarily in an air brake vehicle?
choices: It overheats only the front brakes
It can use air faster than the compressor can replace it
It causes the parking brakes to release
explanation: Unnecessary pedal cycling can deplete air pressure; if pressure gets too low, braking capability is reduced.

question: For older single air systems, what buildup rate is typically required at idle?
explanation: For pre-1975 single systems, a typical requirement is pressure build from 50 to 90 psi within 3 minutes at 600-900 rpm idle.

question: What is the purpose of a modulating spring-brake control valve?
choices: To apply spring brakes gradually for control if service brakes fail
explanation: A modulating valve allows graduated spring-brake application, useful for controlling the vehicle during certain failures.

question: On vehicles with dual parking control valves, why must emergency spring-brake release moves be planned carefully?
choices: Because ABS is disabled during release
Because the separate release tank has limited air and can be used only a few times
Because front brakes do not work
Because trailer brakes cannot be tested
explanation: Some vehicles use a separate tank to release spring brakes temporarily; limited reserve means only a few releases are available.
